company: logic—but some investors worry that geopolitical conviction and venture returns don't always align. Find out more Barbarians meet their new gatekeepers: 'Wealthtech' middlemen By Alexander Davis, Head of Enterprise Reporting This year has been marked by news of investors trying to snatch back billions of dollars from retail-focused private credit funds that their wealth advisers had invested in on their behalf. Much of the coverage centers on questions about the health of the vehicles' underlying loan portfolios and whether the liquidity limits of the fund structures are suitable for the fickle timelines of retail investors. Far less understood is how registered investment advisers actually source, evaluate and buy into private fund opportunities. Opening up the private markets to individual investors hinges on behind-the-scenes infrastructure controlled by a crowded field of fintech middlemen fighting for a slice of the trillions of dollars of private wealth yet to be allocated to alternatives. Operators of these behind-the-scenes platforms wield outsized power in determining how individuals get access to private markets. Depending on their size and sophistication, wealth advisory firms rely on the platforms in varying ways. What they all have in common: They sit at the intersection of alts and the wealth channel, using financial technology to bring the two worlds together. Leading the cohort of alts marketplaces serving this market is iCapital
dealSummary: logic—but some investors worry that geopolitical conviction and venture returns don't always align. Find out more Barbarians meet their new gatekeepers: 'Wealthtech' middlemen By Alexander Davis, Head of Enterprise Reporting This year has been marked by news of investors trying to snatch back billions of dollars from retail-focused private credit funds that their wealth advisers had invested in on their behalf. Much of the coverage centers on questions about the health of the vehicles' underlying loan portfolios and whether the liquidity limits of the fund structures are suitable for the fickle timelines of retail investors. Far less understood is how registered investment advisers actually source, evaluate and buy into private fund opportunities. Opening up the private markets to individual investors hinges on behind-the-scenes infrastructure controlled by a crowded field of fintech middlemen fighting for a slice of the trillions of dollars of private wealth yet to be allocated to alternatives. Operators of these behind-the-scenes platforms wield outsized power in determining how individuals get access to private markets. Depending on their size and sophistication, wealth advisory firms rely on the platforms in varying ways. What they all have in common: They sit at the intersection of alts and the wealth channel, using financial technology to bring the two worlds together. Leading the cohort of alts marketplaces serving this market is iCapital, a privately held powerhouse with a roughly 80% market share, Cerulli data shows. In addition to its platform for advisers to shop for products from fund managers, New York-based iCapital provides education, manager research and an array of back-office support for administrative supervision of client investments. Trailing behind iCapital are CAIS and Subscribe, which mainly target institutional investors. The platforms are powerful gatekeepers. They determine which fund products to distribute through their own websites and white-label portals custom-built for advisory firms. Using these sites, wealth managers typically choose from a menu of funds that their own firm curates based on a larger set of funds previously vetted by iCapital, CAIS and other intermediary firms. AI dictation apps are taking over offices, and the main casualty is the open-plan workspace's last shred of dignity. [The Wall Street Journal] • Americans are prospecting like it's 1849. With gold near $5,000 an ounce and a president who thinks it can't be tariffed, the only thing missing is a covered wagon. [The New Yorker] • Lawyers say think twice about that AI note taker. The bots capture offhand executive comments that can surface in litigation and may void attorney-client privilege. dealSummary: Blockchain company Digital Asset is raising a $300 million round led by Andreessen Horowitz's a16z crypto at around a $2 billion valuation, Bloomberg reported.

dealSummary: Cowboy Space, a startup developing infrastructure for orbital data centers, secured a $275 million Series B led by Index Ventures at a $2 billion valuation.
Grand Games, an Istanbul-based mobile gaming startup, raised a $70 million Series B led by Balderton Capital.
Eyeo, a Dutch developer of image sensors, secured a €40 million Series A led by Innovation Industries.
Ciridae, the developer of an AI operating system for companies in the home services and industrial distribution sectors, raised a $20 million seed round led by Accel.
Milan-based quantum software startup Algorithmiq raised €18 million in a round led by United Ventures and CDP Venture Capital.
